ai.name: AI & Tech
ai.desc: Models · agent tooling · research · industry moves

stories.head: Claude Fable 5 hits GA — Anthropic’s most capable model for long-horizon agents at $10/$50
stories.brief: Fable 5 goes GA for long-horizon agents at $10/$50, 1M context; Mythos 5 stays limited.
stories.tldr: Anthropic made Claude Fable 5 generally available on June 9, 2026 as its most capable widely released model, built for long-horizon agentic work, with a 1M-token context window and pricing of $10/$50 per million input/output tokens [1]. Claude Mythos 5 shares the same capabilities but is limited to Project Glasswing, and access to both has since been restored [1].
stories.why: You build AI agents on Claude — Fable 5’s long-horizon agentic focus and 1M-token context raise the ceiling on what your agents can hold and reason over, while $10/$50 sets the cost of that headroom.

sources.excerpt: Claude Fable 5 is Anthropic’s most capable widely released model, built for the most demanding reasoning and long-horizon agentic work. Claude Mythos 5 shares the same capabilities and is available only in limited release through Project Glasswing. [...] Claude Fable 5 and Claude Mythos 5 both become available on June 9, 2026 [...] Context window and output: a 1M token context window by default, and up to 128k output tokens per request. Pricing: $10 USD per million input tokens and $50 USD per million output tokens. [...] Access to Claude Fable 5 and Claude Mythos 5 has been restored. See our statement for more information.

stories.head: Claude Sonnet 5 lands as a drop-in 4.6 upgrade — adaptive thinking on, $2/$10 intro pricing
stories.brief: Sonnet 5 drop-in upgrade: adaptive thinking default, new safeguards, $2/$10 until Aug 31.
stories.tldr: Claude Sonnet 5 launched as a drop-in upgrade for Claude Sonnet 4.6, with adaptive thinking on by default, a new tokenizer, and the first Sonnet-tier real-time cybersecurity safeguards [1]. Standard pricing is $3/$15 per million tokens, with introductory pricing of $2/$10 in effect through August 31, 2026 [1].
stories.why: As a Claude agent builder, Sonnet 5 is a near-free swap for your 4.6 workloads — but with manual extended thinking and non-default sampling params now returning 400 errors, retest your agent flows before the intro pricing expires.

sources.excerpt: Claude Sonnet 5 is the next generation of Anthropic’s Sonnet model family. It is a drop-in upgrade for Claude Sonnet 4.6 with three behavior changes: adaptive thinking is on by default, manual extended thinking now returns a 400 error (it was deprecated on Claude Sonnet 4.6), and setting sampling parameters (temperature, top_p, top_k) to non-default values returns a 400 error. This page summarizes everything new at launch, including a new tokenizer. [...] Claude Sonnet 5 is the first Sonnet-tier model with real-time cybersecurity safeguards. [...] Claude Sonnet 5 is priced at $3 per million input tokens and $15 per million output tokens, unchanged from Claude Sonnet 4.6. [...] Introductory pricing of $2/$10 per million input/output tokens is in effect through August 31, 2026

stories.head: Epic ships Lore VCS v0.8.5 — race-condition fix for its scalable open-source version control
stories.brief: Lore v0.8.5 fixes a concurrent-node race in Epic’s scalable open-source VCS.
stories.tldr: Epic Games’ Lore v0.8.5 resolves a race condition affecting concurrent node additions that could expose partially-initialized nodes [1]. Lore is positioned as next-generation open-source version control optimized for unprecedented scalability of both data and teams [2].
stories.why: Your UE5 projects mix code with huge binary assets — Lore’s scalability pitch targets exactly that pain, and a concurrency-correctness fix matters if you are evaluating it for a team repo.

sources.excerpt: Resolved a race condition affecting concurrent node additions that could expose partially-initialized nodes
"Next-generation open source version control" optimized for unprecedented scalability of both data and teams.

stories.head: Kimi Code CLI adds agentic muscle — nested agents and plan mode in v0.26.0, /copy in v0.27.0
stories.brief: Kimi Code CLI ships agent-core-v2 default, then nested agents, plan mode, and /copy.
stories.tldr: Moonshot AI’s Kimi Code CLI v0.24.0 (July 14) began running the local server on the agent-core-v2 engine by default, retiring the experimental flag [1]. v0.26.0 (July 16) expanded the coder subagent with background tasks, todo lists, plan mode, skill invocation, and nested agents, and v0.27.0 (July 17) added a /copy slash command to copy the last assistant message to the clipboard [1].
stories.why: As an agent builder, Kimi’s move to nested agents and plan mode is a competing coder-subagent pattern worth studying against your Claude stack, even if you never switch tools.

sources.excerpt: "Add the /copy slash command to copy the last assistant message to the clipboard" (@moonshot-ai/kimi-code@0.27.0, Date: July 17, 2026). "Expand the coder subagent tool set to include background tasks, todo lists, plan mode, skill invocation, and nested agents." (@moonshot-ai/kimi-code@0.26.0, Date: July 16, 2026). "Run the local server on the agent-core-v2 engine by default — the experimental flag is no longer needed." (@moonshot-ai/kimi-code@0.24.0, Date: July 14, 2026).

stories.head: FlowGraph v2.3 for Unreal — async/latent visual scripting as a Blueprints alternative
stories.brief: Flow Node is async/latent by design, unlike Blueprints where nodes subscribe to delegates.
stories.tldr: FlowGraph’s Flow Node is async/latent by design, unlike Blueprints, with active nodes typically subscribing to delegates [1].
stories.why: For your UE5 work, FlowGraph’s async/latent-by-default model fits event- and timeline-driven logic that Blueprints handle awkwardly — a lighter scripting option worth a look.

sources.excerpt: Unlike blueprints, Flow Node is async/latent by design. Active nodes usually subscribe to delegates.

stories.head: Pixel Streaming frontend v0.1.1 for UE5.8 — restores missing TypeScript declarations
stories.brief: Pixel Streaming frontend patch bundles the previously missing TypeScript declarations.
stories.tldr: Epic Games’ Pixel Streaming frontend UI library patch now includes generated TypeScript declaration files in the published frontend packages [1].
stories.why: If you stream UE5 content to browsers, shipped .d.ts files let you integrate the frontend library type-safely without hand-writing declarations.

sources.excerpt: Include generated TypeScript declaration files in the published frontend packages.

stories.head: Blender-For-UnrealEngine Rev 4.4.7 — fixes non-linear hierarchy rescaling on export
stories.brief: Blender-to-Unreal addon Rev 4.4.7 fixes wrong object rescaling on non-linear hierarchies.
stories.tldr: The Blender-to-Unreal export addon’s Rev 4.4.7 fixes a bug where the export did not correctly rescale all objects when the object hierarchy was non-linear [1].
stories.why: If you move assets from Blender into UE5, mis-scaled objects from branching hierarchies are a silent pipeline bug — this fix protects your import fidelity.

sources.excerpt: The export did not correctly rescale all objects when the hierarchy was non-linear.

rejected.reason: Community GitHub repository by an unknown individual (‘47thtechcorner’) aggregating Moonshot AI specs with no citation to a primary official source; benchmark figures (BrowseComp 91.2, SpreadsheetBench 34.8) unverifiable against any authoritative origin at fetch time — confidence 0.38, below gate threshold of 0.6.
